steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a “Complete the text” (logical inference) question. The decisive clue is Allen’s criterion: “the strongest judicial opinions consider and rebut potential objections.” Discussing philosophers whose views conflict with a judge’s position forces the judge to engage with opposing arguments — exactly what Allen says makes opinions stronger.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — “allow judges to craft judicial opinions without needing to consult philosophical works.”: Self-defeating. Engaging with conflicting philosophers requires more consultation of philosophical works, not less. ✗
  • Choice B — “help judges improve the arguments they put forward in their judicial opinions.”: Follows directly from Allen’s logic: rebutting opposing views (via conflicting philosophers) produces the strongest opinions, which means better arguments. ✓
  • Choice C — “make judicial opinions more comprehensible to readers without legal or philosophical training.”: Out of scope. The text never discusses reader accessibility or non-expert audiences; Allen’s argument is about argumentative strength, not readability. ✗
  • Choice D — “bring judicial opinions in line with views that are broadly held among philosophers.”: Unsupported. Allen’s point is that rebutting opposing views strengthens reasoning — not that it aligns opinions with mainstream philosophical opinion. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
